evidence: goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go validates bindings by namespace/action map and formula (`protocolActionContractID(...)`) while goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go validates only prefix plus a positional `entries[0]` equality check.

Both files implement separate contract-spec builders and validators (`protocolContractCatalogSpecs`/`validateProtocolContractSpecs` vs `startramContractCatalogSpecs`/`validateStartramContractSpecs`) with different invariants, indicating fragmented boundary-governance rules inside the same contracts subsystem.

README.md states quality gates rely on targeted contract tests and path-aware CI checks, but protocol_contracts.go and startram_contracts.go keep validation in passive helper functions (`validateProtocolContractSpecs`, `validateStartramContractSpecs`) with no local enforcement path visible in these files.

Critical contract invariants (ID formula parity, family prefix constraints, exact entry expectations) are encoded in helper validators, so confidence depends on external test wiring rather than self-enforcing runtime/compile-time checks in the contract definition modules.

Configuration mutation/parsing logic is implemented through multiple string-keyed mechanisms: `UrbitDocker.UnmarshalJSON` in `goseg/structs/configs.go` has a long per-key switch, while `goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` has a separate per-key registry/parsing/apply pipeline, creating parallel abstractions for the same domain updates.

`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` combines command adapters, transition step assembly, config persistence, container lifecycle actions, alias validation, and MinIO link orchestration in one file, indicating mixed responsibilities in a central workflow module.

Test orchestration helpers and state-drain patterns are repeated instead of centralized (`drainImportTransitions` logic and import transition collection in both `goseg/importer/importer_test.go` and `goseg/importer/extract_test.go`), and very broad scenario tests bundle many unrelated action paths in single test functions (`goseg/handler/systemsvc/system_test.go`, `goseg/system/wifi_test.go`).

goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go:119 calls broadcast.BroadcastToClients() and drops its returned error, while nearby transition paths wrap/propagate errors (for example schedulePack at lines 514-520).

goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go mixes reusable sentinel errors (errConfUpdateMissing at line 139) with ad-hoc fmt.Errorf("...not configured") closures (lines 251-256), reducing predictable error classification compared with sentinel-style errors in other runtime modules (for example errEventBusFull in goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go:34 and errSchedulePackBusFull in goseg/broadcast/state.go:114).

Acronym casing diverges for the same domain terms across config surfaces: EndpointURL (goseg/config/config_update_schema.go:21) vs EndpointUrl (goseg/structs/configs.go:203), GSVersion (config_update_schema.go:35) vs GsVersion (structs/configs.go:218), and mixed C2c/C2C tokens across fields and contracts.

Tests still reference legacy names in failure text after symbol renames, e.g. makeConfig/getWifiDevice/ListWiFiSSIDs/ToggleDevice strings in goseg/accesspoint/accesspoint_test.go:40,84 and goseg/system/wifi_test.go:48,104,205,249,281,298 while implementation names are buildHostapdConfig/wifiDevices/listSSIDs/toggleDevice.

findings.summary: Contract boundary validation is inconsistent across protocol and StarTram catalogs, allowing uneven enforcement of architecture contracts.
evidence: Protocol validation enforces a complete expected binding map keyed by namespace/action and verifies formula-derived IDs for each entry.
StarTram validation enforces only ID prefix and then compares `entries[0].ID` to `APIConnectionError`, which relies on slice order and does not use keyed binding semantics.
findings.suggestion: Unify catalog validation behind one shared contract-governance helper that validates by stable keys (not positional index), enforces deterministic ID derivation rules, and applies the same completeness checks for every contract family.

findings.summary: Per-family ad hoc catalog builders duplicate boundary logic instead of using a shared architectural abstraction.
evidence: Both files define near-parallel patterns (`<family>Contract(...)`, `<family>ContractCatalogSpecs(...)`, `validate<Family>ContractSpecs(...)`) but with diverging rule strictness and naming strategy.
The duplicated pattern suggests architecture-level policy is encoded in each family file rather than in one module-level contract registry abstraction.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single contracts-registry abstraction (e.g., family descriptor + declarative entries) that centralizes ID construction, metadata defaults, and validation policy; keep family files declarative data-only to reduce coupling drift.

findings.summary: Contract families duplicate validation architecture, creating drift-prone governance boundaries.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go` each define bespoke catalog builders and validators with similar responsibilities but different enforcement details.
Governance in README prescribes a unified compatibility surface across multiple files, yet family validation remains decentralized and manually synchronized.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a shared family contract-definition API in the contracts package (e.g., declarative family specs plus common invariant checks) and migrate protocol/startram files to data-only definitions to keep contract governance logic centralized.

findings.summary: Contract invariant checks appear test-dependent instead of locally enforced.
evidence: Invariant-heavy validation exists in unexported helper functions in both contract files, but these files do not show an intrinsic execution path for those checks.
README quality-gate language emphasizes CI and targeted tests, implying critical contract safety relies on pipeline/test discipline rather than guaranteed runtime catalog construction checks.
findings.suggestion: Wire validation into the canonical contract registration/build path so malformed family specs fail deterministically during package initialization or registry construction, then keep tests as regression coverage rather than the sole enforcement layer.

findings.summary: Config update behavior is split across two independent string-keyed frameworks, increasing drift and maintenance risk.
evidence: `UrbitDocker.UnmarshalJSON` in `goseg/structs/configs.go` manually switches over many JSON keys and parses values field-by-field.
`goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` defines a separate key registry (`confPatchByKey`) with per-field parse/apply handlers and its own value parsers.
Both paths encode overlapping config semantics with raw string keys and duplicated primitive parsing helpers, so adding/changing fields requires synchronized edits in multiple abstractions.
findings.suggestion: Introduce one canonical schema/field-definition source for config keys and derive both unmarshal and patch-apply behavior from it (or retire one path), with typed section-level adapters to remove string-key duplication.

findings.summary: Urbit operations runtime mixes orchestration, persistence, and infrastructure concerns in a single module.
evidence: `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` contains transition routing (`runUrbitTransitionFromCommand`), config persistence mutations, container start/rebuild flows, alias checks, and MinIO link/unlink orchestration.
`runShipConfigTransition` centralizes persistence, cleanup, restart, and post-action policy in one generic function with multiple callback parameters, indicating high orchestration density.
Adjacent runtime seam composition in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go` and broadcast publication calls from this workflow show cross-subsystem coupling concentrated in one file.
findings.suggestion: Split this module into focused collaborators (transition planner, config mutator, container lifecycle executor, external-link coordinator) and keep the top-level command layer as thin composition only.

findings.summary: State/runtime access patterns are inconsistent (global singleton fallback, nil-normalization, explicit injection), obscuring ownership boundaries.
evidence: `goseg/broadcast/state.go` uses a package-global default runtime plus variadic runtime-optional wrappers (`bootstrapBroadcastState(runtime ...*broadcastStateRuntime)`).

`goseg/docker/orchestration/subsystem/subsystem_events.go` also normalizes nil runtimes to a global default (`normalizeDockerSubsystemRuntime`) and exposes both default and runtime-specific entrypoints.
`goseg/session/logstream_session_store.go` uses explicit store receiver methods without package-level fallback, creating a different ownership/injection model than neighboring subsystems.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on one runtime ownership convention (prefer explicit dependency injection with constructors) and deprecate default-singleton fallbacks behind compatibility shims.

findings.summary: Tests repeatedly reimplement event/runtime harness logic and bundle multiple behaviors into broad scenario tests.
evidence: Importer tests duplicate import-transition bus draining/collection mechanics across files instead of sharing one helper utility (`drainImportTransitions` and equivalent channel-drain loops).

`goseg/handler/systemsvc/system_test.go` includes large tests that cover multiple actions and side effects in one function (for example mixed swap/wifi/update behaviors), coupling unrelated assertions.
`goseg/system/wifi_test.go` mixes nmcli parsing/runtime tests with C2C action-processing and service wiring tests in one file, blending separate concerns.
findings.suggestion: Extract shared test harness utilities for event channels/runtime seams and split broad scenario tests into focused per-behavior table-driven tests grouped by subsystem concern.

findings.summary: toggleAutoReboot silently drops broadcast publish failures
evidence: toggleAutoReboot ends with broadcast.BroadcastToClients() and always returns nil (goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go:119-120).
BroadcastToClients is explicitly fallible and returns errors (goseg/broadcast/state.go:107-112), including nil-runtime and downstream publish errors.
findings.suggestion: Handle the returned error in toggleAutoReboot and apply the same transition policy used in schedulePack (e.g., wrap with context or use transition.HandleTransitionPublishError with an explicit criticality decision).

findings.summary: Runtime seam defaults use mixed non-configured error patterns
evidence: runtime_ops defines errConfUpdateMissing sentinel (line 139) but defaultRuntimeStartramOps returns fresh fmt.Errorf strings for equivalent "not configured" conditions (lines 251-256).
Other modules expose stable sentinel-style errors (errEventBusFull, errSchedulePackBusFull) that support consistent classification and errors.Is checks.
findings.suggestion: Standardize seam/dependency-missing failures to package-level sentinel errors (or typed errors) and wrap those sentinels with context, so callers can reliably branch on failure class across runtime components.

findings.summary: Same concepts use conflicting acronym casing and token forms
evidence: Patch model uses EndpointURL and GSVersion (goseg/config/config_update_schema.go:21,35) while persisted config model uses EndpointUrl and GsVersion (goseg/structs/configs.go:203,218).
Tests reinforce mixed naming forms and make surface vocabulary less predictable (goseg/config/config_test.go references EndpointUrl extensively).
findings.suggestion: Define and enforce one acronym convention for Go identifiers (for example URL/ID/API/C2C uppercase) and align config structs, patch structs, and tests to that vocabulary in one migration pass.

findings.summary: Test failure messages use outdated function names
evidence: accesspoint tests call buildHostapdConfig but assert with "makeConfig returned error" and "expected makeConfig validation error" (lines 40 and 84).
wifi tests call wifiDevices/listSSIDs/toggleDevice but message strings still reference getWifiDevice/ListWiFiSSIDs/ToggleDevice (lines 48,104,205,249,281,298).
findings.suggestion: Update assertion messages and test names to current symbol names so failing output maps directly to real APIs and avoids reintroducing deprecated terminology.
